stem: For \( x=t^{2} \), \( y=t^{3}-3\cdot[[k]]^{2}t \), a horizontal tangent occurs where \( dy/dt=0 \). Which of the following is a possible value of \( t \) there?
solution: \( dy/dt=3t^{2}-3\cdot[[k]]^{2}=0 \Rightarrow t=\pm[[k]] \); one possible value is \( [[answer]] \).

stem: For \( x=t^{3}-3\cdot[[k]]^{2}t \), \( y=t^{2} \), a vertical tangent occurs where \( dx/dt=0 \). Which of the following is a possible value of \( t \) there?
solution: \( dx/dt=3t^{2}-3\cdot[[k]]^{2}=0 \Rightarrow t=\pm[[k]] \); one possible value is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( k^2\) instead of \( k\) as the value of \( t\).
This triples the correct value.
This divides by \( 3\) instead of leaving \( t=\pm k\).
This adds one to a genuine solution.
This adds two to a genuine solution.
This doubles the correct value.

stem: For \( f(x)=[[p]]x^{3}+([[b]])x^{2}+([[c]])x+1 \), the tangent line is horizontal where \( f'(x)=0 \). Which of the following is a possible \( x \)-coordinate?
distractor_rationales: This adds the two critical points instead of reporting one of them.
This multiplies the two critical points instead of reporting one of them.
This is off by one from a genuine critical point.
This is off by one from the other genuine critical point.
This is off by one (other direction) from a genuine critical point.
This is off by one (other direction) from the other genuine critical point.

stem: For \( f(x)=[[a]]x^{3}+[[b]]x^{2}+[[c]]x+[[d]] \), find \( f''([[x0]]) \) and use its sign to describe the concavity at \( x=[[x0]] \); report the value \( f''([[x0]]) \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of the \( 2b\) term.
This reports \( f'(x_0)\) instead of \( f''(x_0)\).
This forgets the constant term \( 2b\) entirely.
This forgets the \( 6ax_0\) term entirely.
This is off by one from the correct second-derivative value.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct second-derivative value.

stem: For \( f(x)=[[a]]x^{3}+[[b]]x^{2}+[[c]]x \), find the \( x \)-coordinate of the inflection point (where \( f''(x)=0 \)).
distractor_rationales: This drops the required minus sign.
This forgets to triple \( a\) in the denominator.
This uses \( 6a\) instead of \( 3a\) in the denominator.
This uses \( c\) instead of \( b\) in the numerator.
This is off by one from the correct inflection point.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct inflection point.

stem: For the curve \( x=t^{2} \), \( y=t^{3} \), find \( \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \) at \( t=[[t0]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by \( dx/dt=2t \) a second time, leaving \( 3/(2t)\).
This inverts the correct fraction.
This multiplies by \( t\) instead of dividing by it.
This squares \( t\) in the denominator instead of leaving it linear.
This is off by one from the correct concavity value.
This is off by one (other direction) from the correct concavity value.

stem: For the curve \( x^{2}+xy+y^{2}=[[k]] \), implicit differentiation gives \( 2x+y+(x+2y)\dfrac{dy}{dx}=0 \). A horizontal tangent occurs where \( 2x+y=0 \). Which of the following is a possible \( x \)-coordinate at such a point?
solution: Substituting \( y=-2x \) into \( x^{2}+xy+y^{2}=[[k]] \) gives \( x^{2}-2x^{2}+4x^{2}=3x^{2}=[[k]] \), so \( x=\pm\sqrt{[[k]]/3} \); one possible value is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by \( 3\) before taking the square root.
This divides by \( 3\) without ever taking a square root.
This divides the (incorrect) \( \sqrt{k}\) by \( 3\) instead of taking \( \sqrt{k/3}\).
This has the correct magnitude but reports it as the other (still valid) root, which coincides with a genuine solution and gets guarded out; a nearby offset survives instead.
This is off by one from a genuine solution.
This is off by one (other direction) from a genuine solution.
